author: Resident, Oak Hill neighborhood
text: I strongly support the proposed mixed-use development at the former Millbrook factory site. Our neighborhood has needed walkable retail for years, and the inclusion of 40 affordable housing units addresses a real gap. My only concern is parking — the plan shows 180 spaces for 220 residential units plus commercial. Has the developer conducted a traffic impact study? The intersection at Millbrook and Route 9 is already congested during evening commute.

author: Business owner, downtown district
text: As the owner of a small restaurant on Main Street, I'm worried this development will pull foot traffic away from downtown. We've already lost three businesses this year. The developer's claim that 'new residents will patronize existing businesses' ignores the fact that they're building retail space that will compete directly with us. I'd like to see a commitment to no food service tenants in the new retail spaces, or at minimum some kind of economic impact assessment for existing downtown businesses.

author: Environmental advocacy group
text: The Millbrook factory site has documented soil contamination from decades of industrial use. The environmental remediation plan in the proposal references Phase I and Phase II environmental site assessments completed in 2024, but does not include the full reports. We request that these reports be made public before the planning board votes. Additionally, the stormwater management plan relies on bioretention basins that may be insufficient for the 100-year flood events that have become more frequent. The town's own climate resilience plan recommends designing for 500-year events on brownfield sites.

author: Parent, school district resident
text: Has anyone considered the impact on our schools? 220 residential units could add 80-100 school-age children. Lincoln Elementary is already at 95% capacity and the middle school portables are falling apart. The developer should be required to contribute to school infrastructure through impact fees. Also, the proposed building height of 5 stories is completely out of character for this neighborhood — nothing else on Millbrook Road is over 3 stories.

author: Retired engineer
text: I reviewed the structural plans posted online. A few technical observations: (1) The foundation design assumes Class II soils, but the geotechnical boring logs from 2024 show pockets of Class IV fill material at 8-12 feet depth on the north end of the site. This needs to be reconciled. (2) The proposed green roof on Building B adds approximately 35 psf dead load which isn't reflected in the roof framing calculations I can see. (3) The underground parking structure appears to extend to within 4 feet of the existing storm sewer easement — has this been reviewed with the utility department?

author: Neighborhood association president
text: The Oak Hill Neighborhood Association held a special meeting on January 18th to discuss this proposal. By a vote of 47-12, we resolved to support the development WITH the following conditions: (1) maximum building height reduced from 5 to 4 stories, (2) construction hours limited to 7 AM - 6 PM weekdays only, (3) a community benefit agreement including a neighborhood park and public art installation, (4) dedicated left-turn signals at Millbrook and Route 9 funded by the developer, and (5) first priority for affordable units given to current town residents. We believe this development can be positive for Oak Hill if these reasonable conditions are met.

author: Real estate agent
text: For context, I've sold 15 homes within a half-mile of the Millbrook site in the past two years. Property values in Oak Hill have been flat while comparable neighborhoods without brownfield sites have appreciated 8-12%. This development, if done well, could actually increase surrounding property values by replacing a vacant contaminated eyesore with an active mixed-use district. The affordable housing component should not negatively impact values — research from the National Association of Realtors shows no measurable effect from well-designed inclusionary housing on nearby property values.

author: Commuter
text: Please just fix the traffic. I sit in my car for 20 minutes every evening trying to turn left from Millbrook onto Route 9. Adding 220 more households will make it unbearable. The developer should be required to fund road improvements BEFORE any units are occupied, not after.

author: Historic preservation committee member
text: The Millbrook Woolen Mill building — the main factory structure with the distinctive sawtooth roof — is eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places. Our committee submitted a preliminary determination request to SHPO in December 2025. We strongly urge the planning board to defer any decision until SHPO completes its review. If the building is eligible, federal and state historic tax credits could fundamentally change the project economics and incentivize adaptive reuse of the historic structure rather than demolition.

author: Anonymous
text: This is just another giveaway to developers. The planning board always approves everything. Wake up people.

author: Public health nurse
text: Regarding the soil contamination: the proposal mentions remediation to residential use standards, but I want to flag that children are more vulnerable to residual contaminants than adults. If this site will include family housing and potentially a playground or park, the remediation targets should meet the more protective 'unrestricted use' cleanup standards, not just 'residential.' The difference matters for lead, PAHs, and VOCs, which are all common at former industrial sites. I'd also request that the developer fund post-construction indoor air quality testing in all ground-floor residential units.

author: Local artist collective
text: We'd love to see artist live-work spaces included in the affordable housing component. The mill building's open floor plans and north-facing sawtooth skylights are ideal for studio use. Several mill conversion projects in New England have successfully combined market-rate, affordable, and artist housing — see the example of Mass MoCA's Porches Inn or the Lofts at Harmony Mills in Cohoes, NY. This could give the development a distinctive identity beyond generic mixed-use.
